data.title: Eli Lilly: A Heavyweight Battle Isn't Won In Round 1
data.description:
Overview

I'm previewing Eli Lilly and Company's (LLY) Q1 earnings that are expected to be announced at the end of the month, April 30. Now, I'm going to view this through the lens of a “heavyweight battle” between Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk (NVO).

My last analysis on Eli Lilly was in January. I reiterated my “Buy” rating on the company as it appeared they were positioned for a clean pivot from “scarcity” in the obesity treatment market to “access economics.” A lot of what is going on right now in the obesity market is related to expanding access and increasing volume. It’s essentially a “land grab.” However, the battle is more nuanced than that.

Both pharma giants and their oral obesity drugs have distinct advantages and disadvantages. For instance, Novo Nordisk's Wegovy (semaglutide) pill had a first-mover advantage, as it was launched in early January. And because it shares the same brand name as its popular injectable, it has immediate brand awareness. Meanwhile, Eli Lilly’s Foundayo (orforglipron) was just approved on April 1 and is distinct from Eli Lilly’s popular injectable, Zepbound (tirzepatide).

The marketing plans for both companies are a bit different as well. As you might have heard already, Novo Nordisk is friends again with Hims & Hers Health (HIMS), and of course, Hims & Hers Health is incentivized to market the fact that it has Wegovy in stock. Whereas Eli Lilly continues to focus on LillyDirect, which is its own digital platform that provides direct-to-consumer access.

At least in the early innings, these surface-level nuances likely explain why Eli Lilly's newly launched oral obesity drug appears to be underperforming compared to Wegovy. Following the release of early script trends, at writing, Novo Nordisk stock is up 5%, while Eli Lilly stock is down 4%. But in my view, this is like panicking when the opposing team scores first by hitting a field goal two minutes into the first quarter. It’s just way too early to draw conclusions like “Novo Nordisk is winning the oral obesity treatment fight.”

So, in my following analysis, I explain why it's not yet time to worry about these early trends and what Eli Lilly may do in its first-quarter earnings report to calm investors' nerves.

Foundayo Vs. Wegovy

I want to first get the “height” and “weight” of our competitors. If you look at the chart below, it appears that Wegovy has a slight edge in "mean weight loss."

Now, it's important to note some cross-trial limitations. For instance, these trials may have had different methodologies in determining mean weight loss, or the enrollment criteria could have been different, which could influence weight loss.

So, to me, when I look at roughly 12% (ATTAIN-1) versus 16% (OASIS-4) mean weight loss, I don’t see that much of a difference. But when this is contextualized with ≥20% weight loss rates, Wegovy does appear to have a definitive edge. In addition, Wegovy looks better tolerated, which strikes me as unusual because it is, after all, a peptide.

Now, outside of these numbers, Foundayo does have some specific advantages over Wegovy, but they are not “stats” that will show up on the “TV screen.” For one, Foundayo can be taken any time of day with or without water. Wegovy requires strict fasting. And as I've discussed before, the manufacturability between a small molecule like Foundayo and a peptide like Wegovy is completely different. It is typically much less expensive to manufacture a small molecule versus a peptide.

So, this is not just a "race to sell prescriptions." This opportunity has to translate into a durable gross margin story. This is also an obvious danger in reading too much into these early script numbers.

Let's think about a real-world example to contextualize this. Say your friend told you that he sold 10,000 products from a new business venture. You would probably be impressed at first, but if he later explains that he made no profit, you may lose interest.

And, while Foundayo is not yet approved for Type 2 Diabetes, in the ACHIEVE-3 trial, published in The Lancet, it did beat oral semaglutide at the Type 2 Diabetes doses tested. Of course, conditions like obesity and diabetes often coincide, so slapping a diabetes indication on a label is also key to expanding the obesity market.

Now, I say all of this not to downplay Novo Nordisk's oral Wegovy opportunity. I think it's obvious that they have a massive blockbuster drug there. I'm just pointing out that it's way too early to assume that oral Wegovy is going to materially compress the market opportunity for Foundayo. To be honest, I think there's more than enough room for both drugs.

And something we haven't yet considered is this. Some of these scripts are likely in treatment-naïve obesity patients, but as the tail becomes longer, a lot of these are going to be switchers from injectables. And of course, we already know that Eli Lilly's Zepbound has a major volume advantage over Novo Nordisk's Wegovy injectable.

So, I do believe the "longer-tail setup" favors Eli Lilly. I think the math will eventually flip. It's just a matter of timing. That is, whether the conversion wave shows up in the second half of 2026 script data or gets pushed out further as prescribers wait for real-world tolerability data on Foundayo.

There have been some questions regarding the safety of Foundayo. In its approval letter, the FDA mentioned that it required more data to look for signals of serious risks of retained gastric contents and major adverse cardiovascular events, alongside drug-induced liver injury.

However, Eli Lilly already had plans in place to address these concerns. In fact, the company dropped ACHIEVE-4 top-line results just a couple of weeks after the FDA approval letter. This was designed to answer both the cardiovascular events and liver injury questions. The results did not reveal any cardiovascular or liver-related signals. Unlike Wegovy, as Foundayo is a chemically new agent, real-world tolerability and post-marketing requirements will still require a close watch.

This may be relevant later, but the all-cause mortality HR in ACHIEVE-4 was 0.43. This could meaningfully expand Foundayo’s label if the results are replicated in a separate Cardiovascular Outcomes trial.

There was also some news that came out on April 21 that CVS/Aetna (CVS) declined to participate in the Medicare obesity drug program, and UnitedHealth (UNH) flagged “challenges.” Of course, sufficient Medicare coverage would create a tailwind for both Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk. Some estimate an eligible pool of 20 to 30 million beneficiaries.

The Earnings Setup

I'm going to circle back to my January discounted cash flow analysis. Back then, I was modeling Year 1 as 2026, and my assumptions included $76.65 billion (the consensus estimate at that time) in revenue, 25% CAGR over the nine-year projection period, and 20% free cash flow margins. Using these assumptions, the model spit out Eli Lilly’s intrinsic value per share was $1,169.43. I also ran a sensitivity analysis. For instance, what if Eli Lilly's CAGR assumption falls closer to 20%? In that case, its intrinsic value per share would end up in the mid-800s. And my Monte Carlo simulation varied my base assumptions and spit out that Eli Lilly's 5th percentile, or “downside” price assuming no major changes in key assumptions, was $795.13 per share.

Now, I point this out because, at writing, Eli Lilly's stock price is ~$880. In fact, after peaking at over $1,000/share earlier this year, the stock has struggled, and this has notably coincided with the oral Wegovy approval and launch.

For Q1 specifically, the current revenue estimate is $17.84 billion. Recall that Eli Lilly's Q4 2025 was a blowout (a $1.35 billion beat). It generated $19.3 billion in revenue ($3.2 billion in cash from operations), which was up 43% year-over-year. The company's 2026 guidance from February called for between $80 and $83 billion in revenue, or up 25% year-over-year at the midpoint.

Management did flag some headwinds, like a low-to-mid-teens price drag for 2026 from the President Trump/Most-Favored Nation deal, direct-to-consumer pricing on Zepbound, and lower Medicaid pricing on legacy products.

Now, when it comes to what I'm looking for when the company reports its first-quarter earnings, it's not going to be so much “numbers,” especially for Foundayo, as it was just recently approved. I'm actually going to be focused more on "commentary."

For instance, will Eli Lilly reaffirm its 2026 revenue guidance? If they stay there, it may signal to the market that Foundayo is tracking, but if it's narrowed to the low end, it could be a sign that either pricing erosion or Foundayo uptake is lagging.

It will also be interesting to see if management quantifies Foundayo-specific guidance inside the 2026 guide. And this is relevant because the analyst expectations for Foundayo peak annual revenue have a wide spread. For instance, RBC Capital called for a $36 billion-plus peak, while Bloomberg consensus is at $18 billion by 2030.

Finally, more news on Eli Lilly's next-generation obesity treatment injectable retatrutide will be welcome. There will be many TRIUMPH data readouts this year, and the company, as of the last update, had retatrutide's NDA slated for late 2026.

Valuation Assumptions

Given some changes since January, I thought it would be appropriate to conduct another discounted cash flow analysis. I have posted my key model inputs in a table below with rationales.

With assumptions of 18% base revenue growth (realized 16.7% CAGR) with a "high-growth" or "front-loaded" growth profile and a steady-state 24% free cash flow margin, Eli Lilly's DCF value per share comes out at $970.83, which is flagged as "undervalued" by $90.83, or 10.32%.

Naturally, I like to keep things simple, which is why I designed my discounted cash flow analysis to primarily focus on revenue growth and free cash flow margin assumptions.

What I do after I plug in the numbers is that I look at the assumptions and think about how realistic they are.

In Eli Lilly's case, how realistic is it that Eli Lilly can achieve 27% year-over-year growth in the first three years of my model? What about 18% in years 4 through 6? What about 9% in years 7 through 9? How reasonable is a 2.5% terminal growth? And in regards to free cash flow margin, is a steady-state 24% free cash flow margin achievable?

I think they are optimistically realistic.

Management guided +25% year-over-year revenue growth for 2026 specifically. And the hope is that years 1 through 3 will see consistent beats on the wings of newer assets like Foundayo and retatrutide. And I think the 18% step-down averages during the middle of the projection years capture these launches, as well as an expanding GLP-1 market.

In regard to a steady-state free cash flow margin of 24%, the company's free cash flow margin in 2025 was just 14%, but that was because of high CapEx spending to boost manufacturing. As soon as these plants come online and CapEx normalizes, I suspect that the free cash flow margin percentage should expand towards the mid-to-high 20s.

Per my sensitivity analysis, it looks like the market is valuing Eli Lilly for roughly 16.3% annual growth at a 24% free cash flow margin.

I also like to look at scenarios in the event that my assumptions are materially off. For instance, what if Eli Lilly's base revenue growth drops to 12%? Well, the sensitivity analysis says that Eli Lilly would be worth $676.52 per share. Likewise, what if Eli Lilly's free cash flow margins fall short on average to 18%, while growth remains the same? In this scenario, the sensitivity analysis says that Eli Lilly's stock would be valued at $718.01 per share, which represents 18.4% downside.

Conclusion

The oral Wegovy launch looks genuinely strong, and Novo Nordisk has effectively landed the first punch. But this is a "12-round fight," and the stats that matter for the back half, like small molecule manufacturing economics and the Zepbound switcher pool, are all in Eli Lilly's corner. And as I've already pointed out, there's plenty of room for both drugs in this market. So, from my porch, Eli Lilly is still tracking well in the obesity treatment market.

With that said, there are some 2026 headwinds to be aware of. And should oral Wegovy continue to meaningfully outpace Foundayo, I think that Eli Lilly's stock could see a lot of pressure, especially as oral obesity products are key to expanding the overall market.

Given the change of stock price, my discounted cash flow analysis says the market has already repriced the stock to a roughly 16% base revenue growth scenario, which is a bit closer to my downside case than my base case. This creates a favorable risk/reward skew heading into Q1 earnings, so I feel comfortable reiterating my “Buy” rating.

Risks to my investment thesis include Foundayo underperformance, setbacks in Medicare obesity coverage, hotter-than-expected pricing erosion, future competition beyond just Novo Nordisk in the obesity market, retatrutide timeline slips or unimpressive clinical data, M&A execution risk (i.e., the up to $7B Kelonia acquisition), and a premium stock valuation despite the recent pullback.

data.title: TSMC: The Vital Cog Of The AI Revolution Is Sending A Powerful Growth Signal
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, aka TSMC (TSM), reported its Q1 earnings report last week, and despite a strong report, the stock tumbled post the release.

While the stock has regained some of its momentum in subsequent trading sessions, it has significantly and surprisingly, in my view, underperformed the SOX YTD, with the stock up about 26% while the SOX has gained nearly 43%.

There have been a few concerns surrounding the stock, including the threat of further tariff escalations, and the potential competitive threat from a resurgent (INTC).

The question then becomes, is the reaction to these concerns justified? Or do investors need to take another look at the company, especially after its stellar report?

In this article, I am initiating my coverage of TSM by analyzing its Q1 report with a particular focus on TSM's margin profile and the unprecedented multi-year capex commitment from the hyperscalers. I also deploy a machine learning ensemble model to identify TSM’s current valuation.

A Snapshot of TSM’s Q1 Results

TSM had a strong start to the year, with a solid beat on both the top and bottom lines. More specifically, Q1 revenues came in at $35.90 billion, up 40.6% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $404.5 million. Diluted EPS came in at $3.49 per ADR, up 58.3% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.16. Gross margins were strong, coming in at 66.2%, which represents an expansion to the tune of 746 bps [or 7.46%] q/q. The company’s advanced nodes accounted for 74% of the overall wafer revenues. Operating margins came in at 58.1%.

The guidance was equally strong. More specifically, for Q2, management now expects revenues to come in between $39.0 billion and $40.2 billion, which would translate to a y/y growth of 32% at the midpoint. Management also raised their full-year revenue growth guidance to “above 30%,” from the previous guidance of “close to 30%.” Long-term gross margin floor is now projected to come in at more than 56% compared to prior expectations of 53%, and the ROE target was raised to the high 20s.

Company’s Demand Visibility Is So Strong That Management Even Broke a Foundational Rule

One of the key takeaways from TSM’s Q1 report was the management’s revised guidance on the capex trajectory, which now projects FY26 capex to come in at the high end of the $52-$56 billion range. This implies that the company plans to spend more than 50% of the $101 billion in capital expenditures that the company spent in the last three years cumulatively in a single year. In addition, CEO C.C. Wei suggested that the capex in the next three years will be “significantly higher than the past three years,” which would imply that the total cumulative capex would be at or above $200 billion, an unprecedented figure for the foundry industry.

In yet another sign of the explosive demand, TSM also announced three new N3 fabs – a GIGAFAB at Tainan, a second fab in Arizona, and another one in Japan. For more than twenty years, the company has always stuck to the tradition of not adding additional capacity to a node once it has reached its target capacity. Given that management confirmed that this was a deliberate move when they stated during the earnings call that “we double-check with our customers,” this unusual move is further evidence of a significant demand-pull rather than a supply-push dynamic.

Moreover, during last week’s North America Technology Symposium, the company launched the A13 process and the N2U node and also announced that it will be building the advanced CoWoS and 3D-IC packaging capability in Arizona before 2029. The latter is an important development since it would make the whole packaging process more efficient, especially for its U.S.-based customers [Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL)].

Overall, when these developments are all factored in, it paints a very positive picture for TSM’s long-term growth trajectory. The crucial takeaway for investors is that the company has clear visibility into the 2027-2029 AI accelerator and advanced-node demand pipeline. This thesis is further strengthened by the near $200 billion in capex commitments made by the company over the next three years. TSM’s valuation has been weighed down by concerns surrounding the AI spending. The increased demand visibility should alleviate these concerns, especially from a long-term perspective.

TSM’s Margin Profile Is Impressive, and Intel Is Nowhere Close to Being a Credible Competitor

The other key takeaway from TSM’s Q1 report is the company’s strong margins, which are set to remain strong for the foreseeable future. This was evidenced by the company’s recent announcement that it will hold off on deploying ASML's (ASML) “high NA” EUV machines through 2029. Instead, the company plans to extract more value from its existing EUV tooling, strong evidence of the company’s capital discipline, which should support its long-term margin strength.

Furthermore, management raised the company’s long-term margin floor from 53% to more than 56%, with the new floor reflecting a higher demand for its higher-value advanced nodes. As mentioned earlier, revenues from the advanced nodes [3nm, 5nm, and 7nm] accounted for 74% of the overall wafer revenues. In addition, revenues from High Performance Computing [HPC] saw a significant jump, from 53% of total revenues in the fourth quarter of last year to 61% of the overall revenue in the most recent quarter.

One only needs to look at INTC’s Q1 results, released on April 23, to see the dominance of TSM. While INTC’s foundry revenues grew 16% y/y to $5.4 billion, the segment actually posted an operating loss of $2.44 billion. The commercial ramp of the company’s 18A fabs has also been slower than expected, which suggests that the U.S. chip giant is nowhere close to being a formidable competitor to TSM.

Valuation

My price target for TSM was calculated using the forward P/E multiple approach. To compute the projected EPS, I deployed the same machine learning ensemble model that I used for (ORCL). The model used for TSM’s valuation was, however, adapted to account for the foundry cycle dynamics that are a key driver of the company’s earnings trajectory. More specifically, I used a Gu-Kelly-Xiu style target normalization, which allows me to stack a group of multi-currency peers ((SSNLF) in KRW, ASML in EUR, (UMC) in TWD-ADR) into a single training set without the predictions getting distorted by currency contamination.

Similar to what I did for ORCL, the Elastic-Net model was initially included in the ensemble but was then removed since studies have shown that linear models, unlike tree-based models, are not capable of representing the non-linear feature interactions that drive semiconductor earnings dynamics.

I undertook a walk-forward validation test for all three models to determine their accuracy. More specifically, the forecasting accuracy was determined over 12 distinct windows [last three years]. On each occasion, the model was trained only on the data prior to a specific quarter and was subsequently asked to predict the following four quarters. Critically, data leakage was eliminated by re-fitting the data normalization inside each fold using only historically available data, which ensures that the reported error metrics are genuinely out-of-sample.

The model evaluation metrics are as follows:

Model

LightGBM

Momentum Decay

Despite the Momentum-Decay model having a significantly weaker MAPE, the model was still included in the ensemble as it is the only one that anchors to recent trends, thereby offering the ensemble mean-reversion protection in regime-change scenarios [the tree-based models, which are feature-based, could potentially fail together under such a scenario].

Similar to when I projected ORCL’s EPS, I deployed an inverse-MAPE weighting scheme instead of manually assigning fixed weights. The approach automatically allocates a higher ensemble weight to those models with a lower forecast error.

The resulting allocation was:

  1. LightGBM – 44.70%.
  2. XGBoost – 31.50%.
  3. Momentum-Decay – 23.80%.

These also help to determine the projected EPS in the bear and bull case scenarios, which are defined later. The probability-weighted price target of $451.00 implies an upside of about 18% from current levels. The significant upside crosses my Accumulate threshold. While the risk/reward ratio [upside to base/upside to bear] would suggest a Hold, in my view, the probability-weighted framework is more logically consistent given the wide conformal intervals.

Therefore, I am initiating coverage on TSM with a Buy rating and a twelve-month price target of $451.00 per ADR.

Risk Factors

In addition to the factors outlined in the bear case, there are three additional caveats to my Buy rating. First, it is contingent on the AI capex cycle maintaining its momentum at least through the second half of 2026. Should any of the hyperscalers, whose earnings releases start filtering through from next week, trim their capex guidance by more than 5%, then the base case would have to be revisited.

Second, investors should keep an eye out for any tariff-related news. The investment thesis, as mentioned earlier, assumes the current 15% U.S.-Taiwan tariff framework remains intact; any further tariffs would require a revisit of the base case.

Finally, investors also need to keep an eye out for any developments that take place during President Trump’s visit to China and his interactions with President Xi. Any adverse change to the ongoing U.S. support and commitment for Taiwan could also lead to a sharp correction in the name and would have long-term ramifications.

Concluding Thoughts

TSM had a strong start to the year, with a comfortable beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. The unprecedented capex commitment is demand-driven and is a result of the increased demand visibility, which should alleviate any fears of a slowdown in AI-related spending. Furthermore, despite the capex spending, the company is showing tremendous capital discipline, as evidenced by improving margins and a stronger-than-expected margin guidance. Any fears of Intel (INTC) being a credible challenger to TSM, especially after all the investments it received, were put to bed when the U.S. company reported its results.

From a valuation perspective, the stock continues to remain attractively placed at current levels. As such, I am initiating my coverage on the company with a Buy rating.

TSM is most certainly in a position today where it is no longer just a beneficiary of the AI supercycle but rather is an active underwriter of the supercycle.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan: Less Copper Supply Isn't All Bad
data.description:
Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (FCX) is still suffering from the lingering impacts of the tragedy at the Grasberg mine in Indonesia. The copper miner just cut the output targets for the year, causing the stock slump, but Freeport-McMoRan always benefits from lower output leading to higher copper prices. My investment thesis remains bullish on the stock with copper at $6/lb.

Grasberg Restart Struggles

Freeport-McMoRan just reported Q1'26 results with revenues exceeding 2025 numbers due to far higher copper prices. The company reported revenues of $6.23 billion, actually up 8.7% YoY, while the EPS reached $0.57, and the business is set to collect a $700 million insurance settlement payout.

The copper miner saw total copper production fall to 662 million pounds and gold to only 97 thousand ounces. Freeport-McMoRan regularly hit 1 million pounds of copper production in prior quarters, with 868 million pounds back in Q1'25 before the Grasberg mine tragedy.

Whether due in part to lower copper output from Freeport-McMoRan at the Grasberg mine or just higher demand for copper from electrification, the company did just fine despite the much lower output. The stock slumped again due to signs of further delays in ramping the large Indonesian mine back to full production, but the copper demand isn't slowing down, and lower supplies just lead to higher prices.

The company reported Q1'26 average realized copper prices of $5.78/lb, up from $4.44/lb. The business fared just fine due to these higher copper prices and gold up 50% from just $3,072/oz last Q1.

Freeport-McMoRan is now targeting 2026 copper sales forecast at 3.1 billion pounds, down from a January estimate of 3.4 billion pounds, with Grasberg production at just 0.8 billion pounds. Gold sales guidance was reduced to 650,000 ounces from 800,000 ounces. The near-term production from key blocks is expected to run at about 60% of capacity until upgrades are completed, down from a goal of reaching 85% of production in 2H'26 and full capacity not until late 2027.

The company now forecasts the Grasberg mine reaching 1.6 billion pounds of copper in 2028, where output will generally level off. Freeport-McMoRan has a goal of reaching total production of 4.1 billion pounds of copper in 2028, where EBITDA and operating cash flows will soar.

Big Cash Flows

Freeport-McMoRan will reach EBITDA targets approaching $18 billion and operating cash flows around $13 billion. The key is that these numbers are based on $6/lb copper prices, while each $1/lb increase boosts annual EBITDA by $3.9 billion and operating cash flows by another $3.0 billion.

The positive part is that annual capex is only targeted at an average of $4.4 billion in 2026 and 2027. The company even has a discretionary amount of $1.6 to $1.7 billion outlined to allow for easy reduction in capex if copper prices were to collapse suddenly.

The copper miner is a lot more focused on generating positive cash flows despite the massive market opportunity. Freeport-McMoRan could see free cash flows approach $10 billion with constrained capex.

The current copper price of $6/lb is at record highs, but all the available research continues to point to a deficit in supply opening up. As the IEA forecasts, the copper market faces a major shortage as the expected mine supply of announced projects will currently slip, and new projects can't come online fast enough.

Freeport-McMoRan has a market cap of $88 billion now and recently traded at the all-time high of around $70. The stock trades at a relatively attractive multiple of EBITDA targets of just 5x the $18 billion goal in 2027/28 based on copper prices of $6/lb.

The biggest risk to the story is lower copper prices. The surging demand for electrification projects, from data centers to EVs and renewable energy, could reverse, or technological advancements could lead to less copper usage in these projects.

Freeport-McMoRan could definitely run into more mining issues at Grasberg, and the stock could slump, but a large decrease in copper production just boosts copper prices. The company doesn't have a lot of net debt, so financial issues aren't on the table, but copper prices regularly traded more in the $2/lb to $3/lb price range in prior years, and the probability of the business would slump, sending the stock much lower.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Freeport-McMoRan shouldn't slump on any timing issues with the Grasberg mine ramping back up to full capacity. The copper miner prints money at the current copper prices, and lower output just ensures higher prices.

Investors should use the current weakness to add to any positions.

data.title: VEXC: New Ex-China EM ETF Underperforming Its Peers
data.description:
VEXC Fast Facts

Vanguard Emerging Markets Ex-China ETF (VEXC) was launched on 09/30/2025 and tracks the FTSE Emerging ex-China Index. VEXC has a portfolio of 1018 stocks, a dividend yield of 0.79%, and an expense ratio of 0.07%. Distributions are paid quarterly. After seven months of existence, VEXC is still a small fund ($177 million of assets under management) but already has decent trading volumes ($3.6 million of daily average).

As described by Vanguard, the fund invests in stocks of companies located in emerging markets around the world, such as Taiwan, India, and Brazil, and excludes exposure to China. I will use as a benchmark the Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets ETF (VWO).

Portfolio

About 83% of asset value is invested in large and mega-cap companies, with a focus on Taiwan (37.7%) and notable exposure in India (23%). Other countries are below 8%. Despite China's exclusion, the weight of Taiwan points to high geopolitical risk related to China.

The presence of Taiwan among emerging markets is debatable. South Korea has been considered a developed country by S&P since 2001, and Taiwan surpassed it in GDP per capita in 2025. Both countries are ahead of the E.U., the U.K., and Japan in GDP at purchasing power parity per capita.

The portfolio is heavily weighted in technology (33.1% of assets) and has significant exposure in financials (18.7%). Other sectors are below 8%. Compared to VWO, VEXC mostly overweights technology and downplays consumer discretionary and communication services.
